_meta.authoringNotes: Umbrella overview covering atopic, contact (allergic + irritant), seborrheic, stasis, nummular, dyshidrotic, and perioral dermatitis. Cross-referenced against AAD 2014 atopic/contact guidelines, ETFAD/EADV 2020 position paper, BAD 2018 atopic guideline, Weidinger Lancet 2016 review, Eichenfield JAAD 2014. Prevalence figures cross-checked against NHIS and NEA US data.

h1Title: Dermatitis: Types, Symptoms, Causes, and Treatment
heroOverview: Dermatitis is an umbrella term for a group of inflammatory skin disorders that share three core features: redness, scale, and itch. The label covers at least seven distinct conditions, including atopic dermatitis, allergic and irritant contact dermatitis, seborrheic dermatitis, stasis dermatitis, nummular dermatitis, dyshidrotic dermatitis (pompholyx), and perioral dermatitis. Together they affect more than 31 million Americans at any time and account for one of the top three reasons patients see a dermatologist. Each subtype has a different cause, distribution, and treatment, yet they overlap clinically enough that even experienced clinicians sometimes need a patch test or biopsy to tell them apart. Modern management has shifted from steroids alone to barrier-focused, targeted therapy that controls flares and minimizes long-term skin damage.

keyStats.prevalence: 31.6 million Americans have eczematous dermatitis at any given time (National Eczema Association)
keyStats.demographics: Atopic dermatitis affects 10-20% of children and 1-3% of adults; contact dermatitis is the most common occupational skin disease
keyStats.avgAge: Atopic dermatitis usually starts before age 5; stasis dermatitis after age 50; perioral dermatitis peaks in women aged 20-45
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 230 million people worldwide have atopic dermatitis alone (Global Burden of Disease 2017)

definition: Dermatitis (ICD-10: L30.9) is a broad category of non-infectious inflammatory skin conditions in which the epidermis becomes red, scaly, and itchy in response to an internal predisposition, an external trigger, or a combination of both. The term is used interchangeably with eczema in many clinical settings, though technically eczema refers to the specific morphologic pattern of spongiotic inflammation seen in atopic dermatitis. The underlying biology varies by subtype: atopic dermatitis is driven by filaggrin-mediated skin barrier dysfunction and a Th2/Th22 immune skew with elevated interleukin-4, IL-13, and IL-31; allergic contact dermatitis is a delayed (Type IV) Th1-driven hypersensitivity reaction to a sensitizing chemical; irritant contact dermatitis is a non-immune chemical injury to the stratum corneum; seborrheic dermatitis is associated with Malassezia yeast overgrowth in sebum-rich areas; stasis dermatitis arises from chronic venous insufficiency and pooled fluid in the lower legs; nummular and dyshidrotic dermatitis remain largely idiopathic; and perioral dermatitis is a follicular eruption frequently triggered by topical corticosteroids. Dermatitis is not contagious, but secondary bacterial infection (most often Staphylococcus aureus) is common when the skin barrier is broken.

typesClassification.description: Chronic relapsing dermatitis driven by filaggrin loss-of-function mutations and Th2 immune skew. Starts in infancy on cheeks and extensor surfaces, migrates to flexures in childhood, and presents as hand or eyelid dermatitis in adults. Affects 10-20% of children worldwide.

Delayed Type IV hypersensitivity to a chemical sensitizer such as nickel, fragrance, methylisothiazolinone, neomycin, or poison ivy/oak/sumac. Appears 24-72 hours after re-exposure, sharply demarcated to areas of contact. Diagnosed by patch testing.

Non-immune chemical injury from soaps, detergents, solvents, acids, alkalis, or repeated water exposure. Accounts for roughly 80% of all contact dermatitis cases and is the most common occupational skin disease in healthcare, hairdressing, food handling, and cleaning.

Greasy yellow-white scale on red skin in sebum-rich areas: scalp (dandruff), eyebrows, nasolabial folds, ears, chest. Linked to Malassezia yeast and sebaceous activity. Affects 1-3% of adults overall and up to 35% of patients with HIV or Parkinson's disease.

Brown, scaly, itchy patches on the lower legs caused by chronic venous insufficiency and capillary leak. Affects 6-7% of adults over 65 and is often accompanied by varicose veins, ankle swelling, and risk of venous leg ulcers.

Coin-shaped (nummular = coin) red, scaly, itchy plaques most often on the legs, arms, or trunk. Cause is unknown; may follow minor trauma or insect bites and is more common in dry winter months. Often confused with tinea corporis.

Recurrent crops of intensely itchy, deep-seated tapioca-like blisters on the palms, fingers, and soles. Triggered by stress, sweating, nickel ingestion, or atopic predisposition. Episodes last 2-3 weeks before desquamating.

Red papules and pustules around the mouth, nose, or eyes, classically sparing a thin rim of skin at the lip margin. Most common in women aged 20-45 and frequently triggered by topical corticosteroids, fluorinated toothpaste, or heavy moisturizers.

primarySymptoms: Itch (pruritus) ranging from mild to severe, often disturbing sleep and intensifying after warm baths, sweating, or contact with wool — present in essentially all forms of dermatitis.
Redness (erythema) with poorly defined edges in atopic and nummular dermatitis, or sharply demarcated borders in contact dermatitis that mirror the shape of the offending object or product.
Dry, scaly, flaking skin overlying the red patches, with fine white scale in atopic dermatitis and greasy yellow scale in seborrheic dermatitis.
Small fluid-filled vesicles that ooze clear serum when broken, characteristic of acute eczematous flares and dyshidrotic dermatitis on the palms and soles.
Lichenification — thickened, leathery skin with exaggerated skin lines — develops in chronic areas that have been scratched for months, most often the wrists, ankles, neck, and antecubital fossae.
Painful fissures and cracks, especially on the fingertips, palms, and soles in hand dermatitis, often deep enough to bleed and limit grip or walking.
Brown, rust-colored discoloration on the lower legs (hemosiderin staining) in stasis dermatitis, combined with ankle swelling that worsens through the day.
Burning or stinging rather than itch, more typical of irritant contact dermatitis and perioral dermatitis than of atopic forms.
Honey-colored crust over weeping patches suggests secondary Staphylococcus aureus infection, common in scratched atopic skin and a frequent cause of treatment failure.
Hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation that persists for weeks to months after the inflammation settles, particularly in patients with darker skin types (Fitzpatrick IV-VI).
earlyWarningSigns: Persistently dry, rough patches behind the knees, in front of the elbows, or on the cheeks of an infant
An itchy red rash that consistently appears under a watchband, earrings, belt buckle, or necklace clasp
Recurrent dandruff that does not respond to a standard shampoo and extends to the eyebrows or nasolabial folds
Brown discoloration and itch on the inner ankles in someone with varicose veins or prolonged standing work
emergencySigns: Rapidly spreading redness, fever above 38.5°C, and pain over a dermatitis patch — suggests bacterial cellulitis requiring urgent antibiotics
Clustered punched-out vesicles or erosions on top of atopic dermatitis (eczema herpeticum) — a dermatologic emergency requiring antiviral therapy within hours
Painful peeling of more than 30% of the body surface, mucosal involvement, or fever after a new medication — possible Stevens-Johnson syndrome or toxic epidermal necrolysis
Sudden swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat after contact with a topical product — suspect anaphylaxis or angioedema and call emergency services

causes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in the filaggrin gene (FLG) reduce the production of natural moisturizing factor and weaken the stratum corneum, allowing water loss and allergen entry. Found in roughly 30% of atopic dermatitis patients of European ancestry.

Atopic dermatitis is driven by a Th2/Th22 immune skew with high IL-4, IL-13, IL-22, and IL-31 — the same axis targeted by dupilumab and lebrikizumab. Allergic contact dermatitis follows a Th1 pattern driven by interferon-gamma after sensitization.

Nickel, fragrance mix, methylisothiazolinone, formaldehyde, neomycin, balsam of Peru, rubber accelerators, hair dye (paraphenylenediamine), and plant urushiol (poison ivy/oak/sumac) are the top sensitizers driving allergic contact dermatitis worldwide.

Soaps, detergents, solvents, disinfectants, acids, alkalis, and prolonged wet work strip lipids from the stratum corneum and cause direct chemical injury. The most common occupational cause of dermatitis in healthcare workers, cleaners, hairdressers, and food handlers.

Lipophilic yeast of the Malassezia genus colonize sebum-rich skin and produce free fatty acids that incite inflammation in seborrheic dermatitis. The yeast is part of normal flora, so disease reflects an abnormal host response rather than infection.

Incompetent valves in leg veins raise capillary pressure, allow protein and red cell leakage into the dermis, and trigger the inflammation and brown hemosiderin staining of stasis dermatitis. Aggravated by obesity, prolonged standing, and prior deep vein thrombosis.

causes.cause: Topical corticosteroid use on the face
causes.description: The most common trigger of perioral dermatitis. Even mild topical steroids prescribed for unrelated facial complaints can induce a steroid-dependent eruption that worsens on withdrawal, requiring a slow taper and substitution with non-steroid agents.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of atopy
riskFactors.description: Children with one atopic parent have roughly twice the risk of atopic dermatitis; with two atopic parents the risk is three to five times higher. Shared genetics span asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergy.
Carriers have a 3-5 fold increased risk of atopic dermatitis and earlier, more persistent disease. Variants are most common in populations of European and East Asian ancestry.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational wet work and chemical exposure
riskFactors.description: Healthcare workers, cleaners, hairdressers, food handlers, mechanics, and construction workers have 2-10 fold higher rates of hand dermatitis. Hairdressers' lifetime incidence of occupational dermatitis approaches 50%.
Dry winter air accelerates transepidermal water loss and is the most common trigger for nummular dermatitis flares and atopic dermatitis worsening from October through March in temperate regions.

riskFactors.factor: Frequent or prolonged topical steroid use on the face
riskFactors.description: Predisposes to perioral dermatitis, steroid rosacea, and facial skin atrophy. Even over-the-counter hydrocortisone applied daily for weeks can trigger the eruption.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic venous insufficiency, obesity, prior DVT
riskFactors.description: Strong predisposition to stasis dermatitis. Roughly 6-7% of adults over 65 have stasis dermatitis, rising sharply with BMI over 30 and with sedentary occupations.

riskFactors.factor: Nickel-containing jewelry, belt buckles, watch backs
riskFactors.description: Nickel is the single most common contact allergen worldwide. Pierced ears triple the lifetime risk of nickel sensitization, and 15-20% of the general population is sensitized to at least one common contact allergen.

riskFactors.factor: HIV, Parkinson's disease, or other immune compromise
riskFactors.description: Increases prevalence and severity of seborrheic dermatitis substantially — up to 35% in HIV-positive adults compared with 1-3% in the general population.
Increases neuropeptide release (substance P, NGF) that worsens itch and prolongs flares in atopic, dyshidrotic, and seborrheic dermatitis.

riskFactors.factor: Childhood urban living and small family size
riskFactors.description: Consistent with the hygiene hypothesis, urban upbringing and reduced early microbial exposure correlate with higher atopic dermatitis prevalence in epidemiologic cohorts.

affectedDemographics: Atopic dermatitis affects 10-20% of children and 1-3% of adults globally; prevalence has tripled in industrialized countries since the 1960s
Black and Asian children in the US and UK have 1.5-2 fold higher rates of atopic dermatitis than white children, with more severe disease and post-inflammatory pigmentation
Women account for roughly 60% of hand dermatitis cases and the vast majority of perioral dermatitis cases (peak age 20-45)
Stasis dermatitis affects 6-7% of adults over age 65; rare under age 50 unless associated with prior deep vein thrombosis or congenital venous malformation
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of dermatitis is primarily clinical, built on the morphology of the rash, its distribution on the body, the time course of flares, and a careful history of exposures, family atopy, and prior treatments. A well-defined eruption on the wrist under a watch points to allergic contact dermatitis from nickel; symmetric flexural itchy patches in a child with asthma and a family history point to atopic dermatitis; greasy scale on the scalp and nasolabial folds in an adult points to seborrheic dermatitis; brown ankle staining with edema in an older adult points to stasis dermatitis. Where the picture is ambiguous, four investigations resolve most cases. Patch testing with an internationally standardized series (such as the North American 80 Comprehensive Series or the European Baseline Series) identifies the specific allergen in contact dermatitis and remains the gold standard, with patches applied for 48 hours and readings at 48 and 96 hours. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) microscopy or fungal culture rules out tinea, the most common mimic of nummular dermatitis. Total and specific IgE plus skin prick testing supports the diagnosis of atopic dermatitis with relevant aeroallergen or food sensitizations, although results must be interpreted alongside clinical correlation rather than as standalone diagnostics. A 4 mm punch skin biopsy is reserved for unusual or treatment-resistant cases and can distinguish dermatitis from psoriasis, cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, and pemphigoid. Dermoscopy helps differentiate seborrheic dermatitis from psoriasis at the bedside, and venous duplex ultrasound confirms chronic venous insufficiency in suspected stasis dermatitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for diagnosing allergic contact dermatitis. Identifies the specific chemical sensitizer from among 80+ common allergens, allowing targeted avoidance.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small panels of allergen-loaded chambers are taped to the upper back for 48 hours. The patient avoids bathing, exercise, and back exposure during this period. The patches are removed at the dermatologist's office and read at 48 and 96 hours; a third reading at day 7 catches delayed reactions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quickly excludes superficial fungal infection (tinea corporis, tinea pedis, tinea cruris), which can closely mimic nummular and atopic dermatitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician gently scrapes scale from the edge of the rash onto a glass slide, adds a drop of KOH, and views it under a microscope within minutes. Painless; result available the same visit.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Resolves diagnostic uncertainty when the rash does not respond to standard treatment or has atypical features. Distinguishes dermatitis from psoriasis, cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, bullous pemphigoid, and lupus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small disc of skin is removed under local anesthetic with a 4 mm punch tool; a single stitch is placed. Results return in 7-14 days. Mild soreness for a day; a small scar at the biopsy site.

diagnosticTests.test: Total and specific IgE, skin prick testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports the diagnosis of atopic dermatitis and identifies relevant aeroallergens (dust mite, animal dander, grass, mold) or food allergens (egg, milk, peanut in children) that may drive flares.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Total IgE is a routine blood test. Skin prick testing involves placing small drops of allergens on the forearm and lightly pricking the skin; wheal size is measured at 15 minutes. Painless to mildly itchy; results same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Bedside, non-invasive examination of skin lesions under polarized magnification. Helps distinguish dermatitis from psoriasis (regular dotted vessels), tinea, and rosacea.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician applies a handheld dermatoscope to the skin for a few seconds. No discomfort; results discussed at the same visit.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms chronic venous insufficiency underlying stasis dermatitis and assesses for varicose veins, valvular incompetence, and prior deep vein thrombosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A vascular technician applies gel and an ultrasound probe to both legs while the patient stands and lies down. Painless; takes 30-45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies secondary Staphylococcus aureus or streptococcal infection in weeping or crusted dermatitis, especially when flares fail to respond to topical anti-inflammatories.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cotton swab is rolled across the most actively weeping area and sent to microbiology. Results return in 48-72 hours with antibiotic sensitivities.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of dermatitis is structured in tiers that match disease severity, and the same general principles apply across subtypes. The foundation is daily emollient use to repair the skin barrier — patients with atopic, contact, nummular, dyshidrotic, and stasis dermatitis benefit from at least 250-500 g of fragrance-free ceramide-containing moisturizer per week, applied within three minutes of bathing while the skin is still damp. Topical corticosteroids of appropriate potency for the site (mild on face and skin folds, moderate to potent on trunk and limbs) are first-line anti-inflammatory therapy and are used in short bursts of 1-2 weeks to clear flares. For sensitive areas such as the face, eyelids, and skin folds, topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.03% or 0.1% ointment, pimecrolimus 1% cream) and the PDE-4 inhibitor crisaborole 2% ointment are steroid-sparing alternatives suitable for long-term twice-weekly maintenance. The topical JAK inhibitor ruxolitinib 1.5% cream was approved by the US FDA in 2021 for mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis in patients aged 12 and older. Sedating antihistamines (hydroxyzine, doxepin) help itch-driven sleep disturbance but do not act directly on the inflammation. For moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis that fails topical therapy, narrowband UVB phototherapy or systemic agents are added: traditional immunosuppressants include cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day for 3-6 months), methotrexate (10-25 mg weekly), and azathioprine (1-3 mg/kg/day). The newer biologics dupilumab (an IL-4/IL-13 receptor antagonist), tralokinumab (anti-IL-13), and lebrikizumab (anti-IL-13) are now first-line systemic options for adults and adolescents with moderate-to-severe disease, with response rates of 60-75% and a favorable safety profile. Oral JAK inhibitors upadacitinib, abrocitinib, and baricitinib offer rapid itch relief but carry boxed warnings for thrombosis and major adverse cardiac events in older patients. Seborrheic dermatitis responds best to antifungal shampoos and creams containing ketoconazole 2% or ciclopirox 1% plus short courses of mild topical steroid for flares. Stasis dermatitis requires the same anti-inflammatory ladder plus graduated compression stockings, leg elevation, and treatment of underlying venous insufficiency. Allergen avoidance — informed by patch testing — is the single most effective intervention for allergic contact dermatitis and often produces durable remission.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1% to clobetasol 0.05%)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anti-inflammatory therapy for flares. Potency is matched to body site — mild (hydrocortisone, desonide) on face and folds, moderate (mometasone, triamcinolone) on limbs and trunk, potent (betamethasone, clobetasol) on thickened or palmar plantar skin. Used as short 1-2 week bursts and then tapered.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clears 70-85% of mild-to-moderate flares within 2 weeks across all dermatitis subtypes.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.03% / 0.1% ointment, pimecrolimus 1% cream)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing immunomodulators for face, eyelids, neck, and skin folds. Suitable for long-term twice-weekly maintenance to prevent flares. Initial 1-2 weeks of mild burning is common and self-limited.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clear 50-70% of flares; reduce annual flare count by roughly 50% when used as twice-weekly maintenance on previously affected sites.

medicalTreatments.name: Crisaborole 2% ointment (PDE-4 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: Non-steroid topical for mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis in patients aged 3 months and older. Applied twice daily, suitable for the face and folds. The main side effect is application-site stinging in the first few days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clear or almost clear skin in 30-35% of patients at 4 weeks in registration trials (AD-301, AD-302).

medicalTreatments.name: Ruxolitinib 1.5% cream (topical JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved 2021 for short-term mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis in patients 12 and older. Applied twice daily for up to 8 weeks; carries boxed warning class effect for systemic JAK inhibitors. Useful when calcineurin inhibitors and crisaborole are insufficient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 53-54% achieve clear or almost clear skin at 8 weeks in TRuE-AD1 and TRuE-AD2 trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Antifungal shampoos and creams (ketoconazole 2%, ciclopirox 1%, selenium sulfide 2.5%)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy for seborrheic dermatitis of the scalp, face, and chest. Used 2-3 times weekly as a shampoo or daily as a cream during flares, then maintained 1-2 times weekly to prevent recurrence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clears 70-80% of seborrheic dermatitis flares within 4 weeks; maintenance reduces recurrence by roughly 60%.

medicalTreatments.name: Dupilumab (300 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody blocking the shared IL-4 receptor alpha subunit, dampening IL-4 and IL-13 signaling. Approved for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis in patients 6 months and older. Conjunctivitis is the most common side effect (10-25%); does not require routine bloodwork.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EASI-75 (75% reduction in disease severity) achieved in 51-69% of adults at 16 weeks (SOLO 1 and SOLO 2 trials).

medicalTreatments.name: Tralokinumab and lebrikizumab (anti-IL-13 biologics)
medicalTreatments.description: Selective IL-13 blockers approved for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis in adults and adolescents. Subcutaneous dosing every 2-4 weeks. Lower conjunctivitis rates than dupilumab in some comparative data.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EASI-75 achieved in 33-43% at 16 weeks (ECZTRA 1/2 for tralokinumab; ADvocate 1/2 for lebrikizumab).

medicalTreatments.name: Oral JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib 15-30 mg, abrocitinib 100-200 mg, baricitinib 2-4 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid-acting oral therapy for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis when biologics are insufficient or impractical. Boxed warnings for thrombosis, malignancy, and major adverse cardiac events; requires baseline tuberculosis, hepatitis, and lipid screening.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EASI-75 achieved in 60-72% at 16 weeks; itch relief within 1-2 days of starting.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day for 3-6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitor used as short-term rescue for severe atopic dermatitis flares before transitioning to a biologic or longer-acting agent. Requires monthly blood pressure and creatinine monitoring; not for long-term use due to renal toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant clinical improvement in 70-85% of patients within 6-8 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Tetracycline-class antibiotics (doxycycline 100 mg daily, minocycline 100 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line systemic therapy for perioral dermatitis. Given for 6-12 weeks alongside discontinuation of any topical steroid. Macrolides (erythromycin) are used in pregnancy and in children under 8.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clears 80-90% of perioral dermatitis within 8-12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Compression therapy (20-30 mmHg or 30-40 mmHg graduated stockings)
medicalTreatments.description: Foundation of stasis dermatitis management. Worn from morning to evening; combined with leg elevation and weight management. Treatment of underlying venous reflux (endovenous ablation, sclerotherapy) is added in moderate-to-severe cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces stasis dermatitis activity in 60-70% of adherent patients and lowers the risk of venous leg ulcer recurrence by approximately 50%.

surgicalOptions.name: Endovenous ablation (laser or radiofrequency)
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive procedure that closes incompetent superficial veins driving stasis dermatitis. Performed under local anesthetic by vascular surgery or interventional radiology; recovery in 1-2 days.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 85-95% vein closure at 1 year, with measurable improvement in stasis dermatitis and prevention of venous ulcer recurrence in two-thirds of treated patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Skin grafting for chronic venous ulcers
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for non-healing venous leg ulcers complicating long-standing stasis dermatitis. Combined with ongoing compression therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Healing rates of 60-80% at 6 months when paired with compression and venous reflux treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wet wrap therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Two-layer dressing (damp inner, dry outer) applied over emollient and a thin layer of mid-potency topical steroid for 2-12 hours. Particularly effective in moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis flares in children. Reduces water loss, calms itch, and speeds re-epithelialization.

alternativeTreatments.name: Narrowband UVB phototherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: 311 nm light delivered 2-3 times per week in a dermatology clinic. Effective for moderate atopic dermatitis, generalized nummular dermatitis, and refractory hand dermatitis. Typical course 20-40 sessions over 3-6 months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bleach baths
alternativeTreatments.description: Diluted sodium hypochlorite baths (1/4 to 1/2 cup of household bleach in a full bathtub, twice weekly) reduce Staphylococcus aureus skin colonization in atopic dermatitis and lower flare frequency. Endorsed by the AAD 2014 guideline as an evidence-based adjunct.

alternativeTreatments.name: Coal tar preparations
alternativeTreatments.description: Used for over a century in seborrheic dermatitis and chronic plaque dermatitis. Slow-acting but durable; the odor and staining limit modern uptake. Available as shampoos, creams, and bath additives.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be involved when dermatitis does not clear within 4-6 weeks of appropriate first-line topical therapy, when patch testing is needed to identify a contact allergen, when systemic therapy or a biologic is being considered, when the diagnosis is uncertain or atypical, or when secondary infection complicates management. Pediatric atopic dermatitis with food allergy concerns is often co-managed with an allergist, and stasis dermatitis benefits from input from vascular surgery for definitive venous treatment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in dermatology and clinic experience with patch testing using a comprehensive 80+ allergen panel rather than the limited 35-allergen TRUE Test alone. For moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis, ask whether the practice prescribes biologics (dupilumab, tralokinumab, lebrikizumab) and JAK inhibitors in line with current guidelines, and whether nurse-led emollient training and wet-wrap demonstrations are available for children. Continuity matters — most dermatitis is a long-term condition managed over years.
preventionStrategies: Apply a fragrance-free, ceramide-rich emollient at least twice daily and within three minutes of bathing — the single most effective long-term measure across all dermatitis subtypes
Identify and avoid known allergens after patch testing; keep a written list, audit personal care products for the offending chemical, and use a smartphone allergen-scanning app
Use lukewarm rather than hot water for bathing, and limit baths and showers to 10 minutes to reduce barrier disruption
Wear cotton or moisture-wicking gloves under rubber gloves for wet work, and change them before they become damp inside
Treat seborrheic dermatitis maintenance with ketoconazole or ciclopirox shampoo 1-2 times weekly even when the scalp is clear, to prevent recurrence
Wear graduated compression stockings during prolonged standing or sitting if you have varicose veins or prior stasis dermatitis
lifestyleModifications: Replace soap with a syndet (synthetic detergent) or oil-based cleanser; bar soaps and foaming washes strip lipids and worsen barrier function
Use a humidifier in winter to keep indoor humidity at 40-50% — particularly helpful for atopic, nummular, and dyshidrotic dermatitis flares
Wash new clothing before wearing to remove formaldehyde resins and dye residues that can sensitize the skin
Choose cotton, silk, or bamboo fabrics next to the skin; avoid wool and synthetic fabrics that trap heat and aggravate itch
Keep fingernails short and consider cotton gloves at night to reduce scratch damage during sleep in children and adults with severe nocturnal itch
Manage stress through cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness, or paced breathing — well-controlled trials show measurable reductions in flare frequency and severity

recommended: A balanced Mediterranean-style diet with vegetables, fruit, whole grains, oily fish, and nuts — provides omega-3 fatty acids that may modestly reduce inflammation
Adequate vitamin D, especially in winter and at higher latitudes — observational studies link low vitamin D with more severe atopic dermatitis
Probiotic-rich foods such as yogurt and kefir if tolerated; pregnancy and infancy supplementation has limited evidence for prevention of childhood atopic dermatitis
Plenty of water — adequate hydration supports general skin function, though it does not replace topical emollients
avoid: Routine elimination diets in adults without proven food allergy — they rarely improve adult atopic dermatitis and can cause nutritional harm
Foods that have triggered immediate (within 2 hours) hives, swelling, or anaphylaxis on skin prick or specific IgE testing — most often egg, milk, peanut, soy, or wheat in children
Excessive alcohol intake — exacerbates seborrheic dermatitis and rosacea-like flares, including perioral dermatitis
Large amounts of high-nickel foods (chocolate, oats, soy, canned foods, shellfish) in nickel-allergic patients with persistent dyshidrotic hand dermatitis

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise improves overall skin health and metabolic risk factors that contribute to stasis dermatitis. Shower with a gentle cleanser and reapply emollient immediately after exercise to remove sweat that aggravates atopic, seborrheic, and dyshidrotic flares. During severe flares, avoid heat-trapping clothing and consider swimming in chlorinated pools only with a pre- and post-swim emollient or barrier ointment.
dailyManagement: Apply emollient to the whole skin twice daily, and to flare-prone areas more often — total weekly use of 250-500 g for an adult is a useful benchmark
Identify trigger patterns (new soap, new detergent, occupational exposure, stress, dry climate) and keep a brief flare diary for the first 3 months
Treat flares early with the prescribed topical anti-inflammatory rather than waiting — early treatment reduces the total steroid dose required
Avoid scratching: apply a cool compress, pinch, or tap the itchy area instead, and address night-time itch with antihistamines or melatonin if sleep is disrupted
Audit personal care products against any positive patch test results and switch to allergen-free alternatives; many manufacturers publish 'safe lists' for common sensitizers
prognosis: Prognosis depends heavily on the subtype. Atopic dermatitis improves substantially by adolescence in roughly 60% of children, although hand and eyelid involvement may persist into adult life and the underlying atopic tendency is lifelong. Allergic contact dermatitis often goes into durable remission once the offending allergen is identified by patch testing and avoided. Irritant contact dermatitis resolves over 8-12 weeks of strict avoidance plus emollient and short-course steroid therapy, but recurs quickly with re-exposure. Seborrheic dermatitis follows a chronic relapsing course controlled, not cured, by antifungal maintenance. Stasis dermatitis improves with compression and venous treatment but tends to recur if compression is stopped. Nummular and dyshidrotic dermatitis follow an unpredictable relapsing course over years. Perioral dermatitis typically clears completely within 8-12 weeks of stopping topical steroids and starting oral tetracyclines, and most patients remain clear thereafter. Quality-of-life impact is substantial in moderate-to-severe disease — atopic dermatitis carries a disease burden comparable to type 1 diabetes — but modern biologics and JAK inhibitors have transformed outcomes for the most affected patients.
recoveryTimeline: Mild flares of atopic, contact, or seborrheic dermatitis settle within 1-2 weeks of starting appropriate topical therapy. Moderate flares typically clear within 4-6 weeks. Patients started on dupilumab or tralokinumab usually notice itch reduction within 2 weeks and visible skin clearing by 8-12 weeks, with maximum benefit at 16-24 weeks. Stasis dermatitis improves over 6-12 weeks of consistent compression and topical treatment. Perioral dermatitis clears over 8-12 weeks once topical steroids are stopped and oral antibiotics started — patients should be warned that the rash flares for the first 1-2 weeks of withdrawal before improving.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection with Staphylococcus aureus producing honey-colored crust, weeping, and pain — common in scratched atopic dermatitis and a frequent cause of treatment failure

Eczema herpeticum — disseminated herpes simplex infection over atopic skin, presenting as clustered punched-out vesicles, fever, and lymphadenopathy; a dermatologic emergency requiring intravenous antiviral therapy
Chronic post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation, especially in patients with skin types IV-VI, lasting months after the inflammation settles
Venous leg ulcers complicating advanced stasis dermatitis, with poor healing and high recurrence rates without adequate compression
Hand dermatitis causing occupational disability — hairdressers, healthcare workers, and food handlers may be forced to change careers
Sleep disturbance, anxiety, depression, and impaired school or work performance in moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis, with quality-of-life scores comparable to chronic kidney disease

supportResources.name: National Eczema Association (US)
supportResources.description: Patient education, product seal-of-acceptance program, and advocacy for the 31 million Americans with eczematous dermatitis.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Eczema Resource Center
supportResources.description: Authoritative dermatologist-written patient information aligned with the AAD 2014 atopic dermatitis guideline series.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Patient Information Leaflets
supportResources.description: Peer-reviewed leaflets on atopic, contact, seborrheic, stasis, and perioral dermatitis from the UK's national dermatology society.

supportResources.name: DermNet (New Zealand)
supportResources.description: Free, image-rich dermatology reference with detailed pages on every subtype of dermatitis, used by clinicians and patients worldwide.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis produces well-demarcated red plaques with thick silvery scale, classically over elbows, knees, scalp, and lower back. Dermatitis has poorly defined edges, finer scale, more itch than psoriasis, and frequently weeps or vesiculates during flares. Nail pitting and oil spots favor psoriasis. Skin biopsy distinguishes the two when needed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fungal Skin and Nail Infection (Tinea)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinea typically presents as a single ring-shaped patch with central clearing and an active red scaly edge; KOH microscopy or fungal culture shows hyphae. Nummular dermatitis is the most common mimic — multiple coin-shaped patches without central clearing and a negative KOH point away from tinea.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rosacea produces persistent central facial redness, flushing, and acne-like papules and pustules, often with telangiectasia. It spares the area immediately around the lips and does not produce the satellite papules around the mouth and nose typical of perioral dermatitis. Itch is usually absent in rosacea.

confusedWithConditions.name: Urticaria (Hives)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Urticaria produces transient itchy wheals that individually last under 24 hours and move from site to site, with no scale or weeping. Dermatitis lesions stay put for days to weeks, develop scale, and frequently become lichenified with chronic scratching. Acute urticaria points to a histamine-driven allergic reaction rather than eczematous inflammation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lichen Planus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lichen planus presents as polygonal, flat-topped, violaceous, intensely itchy papules, classically on the wrists, ankles, and inner forearms, often with white lacy Wickham striae and oral involvement. The morphology and distribution differ from the broader, scalier patches of dermatitis.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common chronic form of dermatitis and the prototype eczematous disease in this umbrella category.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Delayed Type IV hypersensitivity form of dermatitis diagnosed by patch testing; one of the seven subtypes covered here.

relatedConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Malassezia-associated dermatitis affecting sebum-rich skin; the most common cause of dandruff and facial scale in adults.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Eczema is the commonly used synonym for the spongiotic inflammation pattern that defines several forms of dermatitis.

relatedConditions.name: Occupational Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Hand and forearm dermatitis from workplace irritants and allergens; the most common occupational skin disease.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Part of the atopic march alongside atopic dermatitis, allergic rhinitis, and food allergy; up to 50% of children with atopic dermatitis go on to develop asthma.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever)
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs with atopic dermatitis in around 60% of affected adults; shares the Th2 immune skew.

faqs.question: What is dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Dermatitis is an umbrella term for inflammatory skin conditions that share redness, scale, and itch. It includes atopic dermatitis (eczema), allergic and irritant contact dermatitis, seborrheic dermatitis, stasis dermatitis, nummular dermatitis, dyshidrotic dermatitis, and perioral dermatitis. Each subtype has a different cause and treatment, but all involve inflammation of the upper layers of the skin and are not contagious.

faqs.question: What is the difference between dermatitis and eczema?
faqs.answer: The two words are often used interchangeably. Eczema technically refers to the spongiotic inflammation pattern seen under the microscope, while dermatitis is the broader clinical term for any inflamed, itchy, scaly skin. Atopic dermatitis is the most common form and is what most people mean when they say eczema, but contact, seborrheic, and other forms are also dermatitis.

faqs.question: Is dermatitis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Dermatitis is an inflammatory condition driven by the immune system, barrier dysfunction, or chemical contact, not by infection. You cannot catch it from another person. Secondary bacterial or viral infection on top of dermatitis can be contagious, which is why honey-colored crust or grouped vesicles on a flare should be checked by a clinician.

faqs.question: What causes dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Causes depend on the subtype. Atopic dermatitis arises from filaggrin gene variants and a Th2 immune skew, contact dermatitis from chemical allergens or irritants, seborrheic dermatitis from Malassezia yeast and sebaceous activity, stasis dermatitis from chronic venous insufficiency, and perioral dermatitis often from prolonged topical steroid use on the face. Family history, dry climate, and stress influence all forms.

faqs.question: What does dermatitis look like?
faqs.answer: Most dermatitis presents as red, scaly, itchy patches with poorly defined edges. Acute flares may weep clear fluid or form small blisters; chronic patches become thickened and leathery from scratching. Contact dermatitis tends to be sharply demarcated to the area of contact, while atopic dermatitis favors the elbow and knee creases in children and the hands and eyelids in adults.

faqs.question: How is dermatitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is mainly clinical, based on the appearance, distribution, and history of the rash. Patch testing identifies the allergen in contact dermatitis. KOH microscopy excludes fungal infection. A skin biopsy is used when the diagnosis is uncertain or when the rash does not respond to standard treatment. Allergy blood tests and skin prick testing support the diagnosis of atopic dermatitis with relevant allergens.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for dermatitis?
faqs.answer: The best treatment depends on the type and severity. The foundation is daily fragrance-free emollient and avoidance of triggers. Topical corticosteroids treat flares, while calcineurin inhibitors and crisaborole are steroid-sparing options for the face and folds. Moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis is now treated with biologics such as dupilumab or oral JAK inhibitors. Seborrheic dermatitis responds to antifungal shampoos.

faqs.question: Can dermatitis be cured?
faqs.answer: Most forms of dermatitis are controlled rather than cured. Allergic contact dermatitis can go into durable remission once the offending allergen is identified and avoided. Atopic dermatitis often improves substantially by adolescence in around 60% of children but may persist into adult life. Seborrheic, stasis, nummular, and dyshidrotic dermatitis follow chronic relapsing courses that are kept under control with maintenance treatment.

faqs.question: How long does dermatitis take to clear?
faqs.answer: Mild flares typically settle within 1-2 weeks of starting topical corticosteroid or calcineurin inhibitor therapy. Moderate flares clear within 4-6 weeks. Patients on biologics such as dupilumab usually notice itch reduction in 2 weeks and visible clearing by 8-12 weeks. Perioral dermatitis clears in 8-12 weeks once topical steroids are stopped and oral antibiotics are started.

faqs.question: Are topical steroids safe for dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes, when used correctly. Short bursts of an appropriately potent steroid for 1-2 weeks are safe and very effective at clearing flares. Long-term unsupervised use, especially on the face, can cause skin thinning, stretch marks, or trigger perioral dermatitis and rosacea-like eruptions. Following a clinician's instructions on potency, duration, and site avoids most adverse effects.

faqs.question: What triggers dermatitis flares?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include cold dry weather, low indoor humidity, harsh soaps and detergents, fragranced products, wool and synthetic fabrics, sweat, stress, infections, and sleep deprivation. Contact dermatitis flares with re-exposure to the relevant allergen or irritant. Stasis dermatitis worsens with prolonged standing. Identifying personal triggers through a brief flare diary is one of the most useful self-management steps.

faqs.question: Can food cause dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Food allergy is a clinically relevant trigger in roughly one-third of children with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis, most commonly egg, milk, peanut, soy, or wheat. In adults, true food-triggered dermatitis is uncommon. Elimination diets without proven food allergy rarely help and can cause nutritional harm, so testing should guide any dietary changes.

faqs.question: Why does dermatitis itch so much?
faqs.answer: Itch in dermatitis is driven by inflammation-related cytokines, especially IL-31, plus skin barrier disruption and nerve sensitization. Scratching feels relieving but damages the skin further and intensifies itch over time, a cycle called the itch-scratch loop. Newer treatments such as dupilumab and JAK inhibitors target this cycle directly and reduce itch within 1-2 weeks.

faqs.question: What is the best moisturizer for dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Choose a fragrance-free, dye-free emollient containing ceramides, glycerin, or petrolatum. Thicker ointments and creams hold water in the skin better than thinner lotions. Apply within three minutes of bathing while the skin is still damp, and reapply at least twice a day. Recognized seal-of-acceptance programs from the National Eczema Association help identify suitable products.

faqs.question: Can dermatitis affect my mental health?
faqs.answer: Yes. Moderate-to-severe dermatitis is strongly associated with anxiety, depression, sleep disturbance, and impaired school or work performance. Quality-of-life scores in severe atopic dermatitis are comparable to chronic kidney disease. Effective control of the skin disease, combined with sleep restoration and psychological support when needed, substantially improves mental health outcomes.

faqs.question: Is dermatitis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Atopic dermatitis runs strongly in families. Children with one atopic parent have roughly double the risk, and with two atopic parents three to five times higher risk. Filaggrin gene variants are the best-known inherited contributor. Contact, seborrheic, stasis, and perioral dermatitis have weaker genetic links and depend more on exposures and other medical conditions.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regular exercise improves overall skin and cardiovascular health and helps with the weight control important for stasis dermatitis. Shower with a gentle cleanser and reapply emollient after exercise to remove sweat that aggravates flares. Avoid heat-trapping synthetic fabrics during severe flares and consider moisture-wicking cotton or bamboo clothing instead.

faqs.question: What is patch testing and do I need it?
faqs.answer: Patch testing is the gold standard for diagnosing allergic contact dermatitis. Small panels of common allergens are taped to the upper back for 48 hours, then read at 48 and 96 hours. It is recommended when contact dermatitis is suspected, when hand or facial dermatitis fails to clear, or when atopic dermatitis worsens unexpectedly. Identifying the allergen often produces durable remission.

faqs.question: How much does dermatitis treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Most dermatitis is managed with inexpensive generic emollients and topical steroids that cost a few dollars per tube. Topical calcineurin inhibitors and crisaborole are more expensive but covered by most insurance plans. Biologics such as dupilumab list at roughly USD 35,000 per year before insurance and patient assistance programs. Generic allopurinol-style affordability does not yet apply to advanced atopic dermatitis therapy.

faqs.question: When should I see a dermatologist for dermatitis?
faqs.answer: See a dermatologist if the rash does not clear within 4-6 weeks of appropriate first-line treatment, if patch testing is needed, if you are considering systemic therapy or a biologic, if the diagnosis is uncertain or atypical, or if secondary infection is complicating management. Persistent hand dermatitis, facial dermatitis, and any rash interfering with sleep or work also warrants specialist review.

faqs.question: Can dermatitis lead to skin cancer?
faqs.answer: Dermatitis itself does not increase skin cancer risk. Long-term use of certain immunosuppressants, particularly cyclosporine and azathioprine, modestly increases the risk of non-melanoma skin cancer, especially in patients with significant sun exposure. Routine skin checks are recommended for patients on long-term systemic immunosuppression, and biologics such as dupilumab do not carry the same risk signal.

faqs.question: Is dermatitis more common in any ethnic groups?
faqs.answer: Atopic dermatitis is more prevalent and often more severe in Black and Asian children in the US and UK compared with white children, with higher rates of post-inflammatory pigment change. Filaggrin variants are most common in European and East Asian populations. Seborrheic dermatitis is more visible on darker skin, where it can produce striking hypopigmented patches in addition to scale.

metaDescription: Dermatitis covers atopic, contact, seborrheic, stasis, and other inflamed itchy skin disorders. Types, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatments reviewed by doctors.
